name: Braised Pork and Eggs in Coconut Caramel Sauce
intro: Like the Chinese pork belly enjoyed in steamed buns, this southern Vietnamese comfort dish is unctuous and dark mahogany in color. But, instead of relying on sugar, soy sauce, and rice wine, it employs caramel sauce, coconut water, and fish sauce to power its savory sweetness. I typically eat thịt heo kho trứng with rice, breaking the egg into chunks to mix with the pork, and drizzling the sauce over all. Add a simple vegetable like boiled green beans or broccoli, or the zucchini stir-fry on this page, to complete the meal. This is homey, everyday fare, but people love it for Tet Lunar New Year too.

ingredients.item: to 2 tablespoons canola or other neutral oil
boneless fatty pork shoulder, boneless country-style pork ribs, or pork belly (skin on or off), cut into chunks about 1 inch thick and 2 to 3 inches long, each piece with some fat and lean
Scant ¼ cup Caramel Sauce (this page)
fish sauce, plus more as needed
black peppercorns, or ½ teaspoon black peppercorns plus 3 whole star anise (24 robust points)
Thai chiles, or 1 serrano chile, thinly sliced (optional)
small dried round rice noodles (maifun), or 8 ounces dried rice capellini or thin spaghetti (see this page)
Leaves from 1 large head of soft-leaf lettuce (such as butter, Boston, or red or green leaf)
to 8 bushy sprigs fresh mint or basil

method: Pour 1 cup water into a 6-quart pressure cooker or multicooker. Set a metal steamer rack or basket (use a perforated metal insert designed for a pressure cooker or a large collapsible steamer basket) inside the cooker and place the eggs on the rack or in the basket. Lock on the lid, bring to low pressure, adjust the heat to maintain pressure, and cook for 4 to 6 minutes; use the lesser time for runnier yolks. (If using a multicooker, such as a Fagor Lux or Instant Pot, program to Steam for the same amount of time.) Meanwhile, set up a bowl of ice water for cooling the eggs.

When the eggs are done, release the pressure immediately. Transfer the eggs to the ice bath and let cool for 5 to 10 minutes (the longer time is for newer eggs, which are more difficult to peel), then peel the eggs and set aside.

Remove the steamer rack from the cooker and discard the water. Dry the cooker and warm over medium-high heat. (If using a multicooker, use a high heat setting, such as Brown on the Fagor Lux, or adjust the Sauté function on the Instant Pot). Add enough of the canola oil to film the bottom of the pot. When the oil is shimmering, in two or three batches, add the pork and lightly sear on all sides, about 1 minute; transfer the seared pork to a bowl or plate.

In a medium bowl, combine the peanuts, green onions, curry powder, pepper, water, oyster sauce, and fish sauce. Add the beef and mix with your fingers. (If not cooking right away, cover and refrigerate for up to 24 hours.) Form into twenty-four patties, each a good 2 inches wide and ½ inch thick. Set aside.

In large pot of unsalted water, boil the noodles, then drain, rinse with water, drain, and let cool for 5 minutes. Since the noodles are unwieldy, arrange them as 2-inch nests on a plate or in a shallow bowl. Set at the table with the dipping sauce, lettuce, and fresh herbs.

Lightly oil a cast-iron stove-top grill (or lightly film a heavy skillet with oil) and set over medium-high heat. In batches, add the beef and cook for 4 to 5 minutes, turning midway, until medium to medium-well done. (These are usually not eaten medium-rare, but you can cook for less time, if you like.) Transfer to a platter and let cool for a few minutes.
Have diners build lettuce wraps with herbs, noodles, and beef (for easier eating, you can break or cut each patty into two or three bite-size pieces). Dunk in the sauce and eat.
